title: Understanding Your Worth
description: Discover your identity and challenge limiting beliefs about yourself and technology.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama sat on the wooden bench outside her family's compound in Kumasi, staring at the cracked screen of her brother's old phone. On it, a website glowed — colorful, alive, interactive. 'Who builds things like this?' she whispered. Her aunt overheard and laughed. 'Ama, leave those computer things. Focus on what matters — learn to cook well so you can find a good husband.' That night, Ama couldn't sleep. The aunt's words echoed: computer things are not for you. But another voice, quieter but stubborn, whispered back: Why not me?[/STORY]

sections.heading: Inherent Self-Worth
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama woke up early, the sound of sweeping broomsticks outside her window. Her aunt's voice still hovered in her mind, heavy like the morning fog. She walked to the mirror and looked at herself. Was she only meant to follow the path others had paved? She remembered her grandmother's wise words: 'Ama, your value was born with you, like the gold in our land. Nobody can give it to you, and nobody can take it away.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]Self-worth is the deep, unshakeable belief that you are valuable and deserving of respect simply because you exist. It is not something you earn through domestic work, marriage, or approval from others — it is your birthright. When you believe you are worthy of learning technology, you build the foundation for every skill you will learn.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]What is one thing about yourself that you are proud of that does not depend on anyone else's approval? Write it down.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Limiting Beliefs vs. Truth
sections.body:
[STORY]At the market, Ama saw a flyer for a computer coding class. Her heart leapt, but then sank. Her cousin Kwame had once told her, 'Coding is for geniuses and boys. You need a big brain and a special school.' Ama looked at her hands, worn from helping her mother sell vegetables. Maybe Kwame was right. Maybe girls like her weren't meant for computers.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Limiting beliefs are stories we tell ourselves that restrict our potential. They feel like facts, but they are actually fears. The truth is that coding ability is built through consistent practice, not innate talent. The most successful programmers in the world are not geniuses — they are people who practiced solving problems every single day.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Identify one negative thought you have about your ability to learn technology. Write it down, then write the truth next to it.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Rewriting Your Story
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama took a piece of charcoal and a scrap of cardboard from a tomato crate. She decided she was tired of letting her cousin's words dictate her limits. She wrote: 'I, Ama, can learn to code.' She looked at it. It felt bold, almost dangerous. But as she read it aloud, she felt a quiet strength rising in her chest.[/STORY]

[LESSON]You have the power to reject negative social programming. Shifting from a 'fixed mindset' (believing your abilities are set in stone) to a 'growth mindset' (believing you can grow through effort) is the key. Replace 'I cannot do this' with 'I cannot do this YET.'[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write your own Worth Declaration: 'I, [your name], am worthy of learning technology. I will learn step by step.' Keep it close to you.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Reflection Exercise
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama sat by the stove, watching the water boil for fufu. She thought about how she had managed her mother's market stall budget last month when the prices fluctuated. She had calculated the profits, organized the stock, and handled the customers. It had been hard, but she had done it with patience and logical planning.[/STORY]

[LESSON]You already possess the core qualities of a successful programmer. Patience helps you find bugs. Logical thinking helps you write instructions. Determination keeps you going when code breaks. Tech is simply a new language to apply the strengths you already have.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Think of a difficult situation in your life that you successfully resolved. What qualities did you use? How can they help you in coding?[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What is the definition of self-worth?
options: The amount of money you earn
The belief that you are inherently valuable and deserving
The level of education you reach
How popular you are in your neighborhood
quiz.answer: The belief that you are inherently valuable and deserving

quiz.question: Which of the following is a limiting belief?
options: I can learn HTML with practice
I am not smart enough to study computer programming
Mistakes are a normal part of the learning journey
Women can succeed in tech roles in Ghana
quiz.answer: I am not smart enough to study computer programming

quiz.question: How do you overcome a limiting belief?
options: By replacing negative thoughts with effort-focused truths
By waiting for someone else to change it
quiz.answer: By replacing negative thoughts with effort-focused truths

quiz.question: Your potential is:
options: Limitless and shaped by consistent effort
Dependent on what other people say about you
quiz.answer: Limitless and shaped by consistent effort

quiz.question: Why does Pool of Grace start with self-worth?
options: Because building confidence is key to sustained learning and growth
Because we do not teach coding
quiz.answer: Because building confidence is key to sustained learning and growth

title: Breaking Cultural Barriers
description: Explore cultural narratives about women in technology and rewrite your story.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama knew she wanted to join the local tech meetup, but her friends mocked her: 'Ama, why do you want to stay inside all day with screens? You need to find a husband.' They spoke out of concern, but their words felt like heavy chains. Ama took a deep breath. She had heard of a software developer named Akosua who worked in Kumasi. She decided to go find her, hoping to see what was possible.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Cultural Roles and Technology
sections.body:
[STORY]At home, Ama was expected to cook and clean while her brother sat on the computer. She felt it was unfair, but she didn't want to cause trouble. She decided to speak to her mother. 'Mama, if I finish my chores early, can I use the family computer for one hour?' Her mother was surprised but agreed. It was a small negotiation, but it opened a door.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Traditional expectations often steer girls away from technology, positioning it as a masculine field. Obaa tumi bi a, na obarima tumi bi (What a woman can do, a man can also do). Technology does not require physical strength; it requires logical thinking and persistence — qualities you possess in abundance.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Identify one traditional expectation in your household that you can work around to create study time. How will you negotiate it?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Dealing with Active Discouragement
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama's father sat her down. 'Ama, computers are too hard, and there are no jobs for girls in that. Focus on something safe.' Ama felt tears coming, but she remembered Akosua's advice. She showed her father a newspaper article about tech jobs in Accra. 'Papa, I respect your worry. But tech is growing, and companies are hiring female developers. I want to try.' Her father looked at the article, surprised, and went silent.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Active discouragement usually comes from concern or a limited worldview. Learn to listen politely but protect your career path using constructive boundary setting. Share facts and results, and show them your progress rather than arguing.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a polite, firm response to a family member who says tech is 'too hard' for you.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Role Model Vicarious Learning
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama visited Akosua's office in Kumasi. On the wall were pictures of Farida Bedwei, a famous Ghanaian software engineer who built banking software despite cerebral palsy, and Ethel Cofie, founder of Women in Tech Africa. 'If they did it,' Akosua told her, 'with all their challenges, why not you?' Ama stared at the photos. They looked like her. For the first time, she truly believed she belonged.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Seeing women who look like you succeed in technology is one of the strongest builders of self-belief. Psychologist Albert Bandura called this 'vicarious experience.' When you see someone similar to you achieve something, your brain registers: 'If she can do it, I can too.'[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Find or read about one African woman in technology. What is her name, and what did she build?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Reflecting on Cultural Strengths
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama thought about her community savings group, the 'susu.' Everyone contributed money to a common pot, and each member took turns using it to grow their business. It was a system built on trust and mutual support. Akosua told her, 'Software development is just like a susu. We share our code and help each other build better things.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]Not all cultural values are barriers. Ghanaian values of community support, resilience, and sharing are powerful assets in tech. The developer community relies on open source and collaborative forums where people contribute their knowledge to help everyone grow.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]How can you bring the spirit of communal support to your coding journey and support your peers?[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What is a major cultural barrier for Ghanaian girls in tech?
options: Traditional expectations that direct women away from STEM careers
Computers not being allowed in Ghana
A lack of English language skills
quiz.answer: Traditional expectations that direct women away from STEM careers

quiz.question: When someone discourages you from tech, you should:

Agree with them that you cannot do it
quiz.answer: Protect your career dreams and set constructive boundaries

quiz.question: How do role models help build your capability?
options: They do all the coding work for you
They prove that success is possible and show you the way
They do not help at all
quiz.answer: They prove that success is possible and show you the way

quiz.question: Which of the following is an effective way to counter cultural stereotyping?
options: Connecting with female mentors and peer groups
quiz.answer: Connecting with female mentors and peer groups

quiz.question: Empowerment theory suggests that agency is:
options: The psychological capability to make choices and act on them
quiz.answer: The psychological capability to make choices and act on them

title: Building Confidence in Tech
description: Develop self-efficacy and belief in your ability to succeed in technology.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama opened her editor to write her first lines of code. But when she refreshed the browser, the screen remained blank. She felt a wave of frustration. 'I don't know what I'm doing,' she sighed, putting her head on the table. She wanted to quit before she even started. Akosua sat next to her. 'Ama, confidence is not something you are born with. It is a house you build block by block.'[/STORY]

sections.heading: What is Self-Efficacy?
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama felt she wasn't 'smart enough' because she didn't understand the syntax immediately. Akosua explained: 'Self-esteem is how you feel about yourself generally. Self-efficacy is your belief that you can complete a specific task, like styling a button. You don't need general confidence to start coding; you just need to believe you can learn this one step.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]Self-efficacy is the specific belief that you can succeed at a particular task. It is a domain-specific skill that develops through effort, not general self-esteem. Research shows that self-efficacy is a stronger predictor of academic success than raw IQ. Believing you can learn is half the battle.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Rate your confidence in learning technology on a scale of 1-10. What is one specific task you feel confident about?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: The Four Sources of Self-Efficacy
sections.body:
[STORY]Akosua showed Ama the four sources of confidence. 'First, mastery: you code and get it right. Second, vicarious: you watch me code. Third, persuasion: I tell you that you can do it. Fourth, emotion: you reframe your panic as excitement.' Ama wrote these four sources in her notebook. It made confidence feel like a recipe she could cook.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Albert Bandura identified four sources of self-efficacy: 1. Mastery Experiences (successes), 2. Vicarious Experiences (seeing others), 3. Social Persuasion (encouragement), and 4. Physiological States (managing stress). Pool of Grace is structured to provide all four.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Which of the four sources of self-efficacy do you need most today? How can you obtain it?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Engineering Mastery Experiences
sections.body:
[STORY]Instead of trying to build a whole website, Akosua had Ama write just one line of code: a heading tag with her name. Ama typed: `

Ama

`. She clicked save, refreshed the page, and there it was — her name, bold and clear. She smiled. 'I did it!' she said. 'Yes,' Akosua said, 'that is your first mastery experience. Now let's build the next.'[/STORY] [LESSON]To build confidence, break large tasks into tiny, achievable steps (called 'scaffolding'). Every small success builds your belief in your capability. A senior developer at a major company like MTN or Hubtel builds software the same way — one tiny line at a time.[/LESSON] [YOUR_TURN]Break a large goal you have (like learning to code) into three tiny, manageable tasks you can do this week.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Managing Learning Anxiety
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama typed a paragraph tag but forgot the closing bracket. The screen filled with red error text. Her heart raced. 'I broke it!' she gasped. Akosua laughed. 'You didn't break anything. The computer is just having a conversation with you. Read the message.' Ama read it: 'Expected closing tag on line 12.' She added the bracket, and the error vanished.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Anxiety is a natural body response to errors. Reframe it: error messages are not grades or punishments. They are useful diagnostics telling you exactly what to fix. When you see an error, read it out loud to engage your logical brain and calm your emotions.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Next time you get a coding error, read it out loud and explain to yourself what the computer is asking for. Write down this experience.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: Who defined the concept of Self-Efficacy?
quiz.answer: Albert Bandura

quiz.question: Which source of self-efficacy is built when you successfully solve a programming challenge?
quiz.answer: Mastery experiences

quiz.question: If you feel highly anxious when encountering code errors, you should:
options: Frame the error as a helpful diagnostic guide instead of a failure
Restart your computer and ignore the bug
Assume you are not meant for technology
quiz.answer: Frame the error as a helpful diagnostic guide instead of a failure

quiz.question: How does social persuasion support your learning?
options: By providing encouragement and feedback from mentors
By writing the code for you
By testing your software for you
quiz.answer: By providing encouragement and feedback from mentors

quiz.question: To build technical self-efficacy, it is best to:
options: Only read books without typing code
Break tasks into tiny, manageable steps and code daily
Attempt huge, complex projects on day one
Code only when you feel 100% confident
quiz.answer: Break tasks into tiny, manageable steps and code daily

title: Overcoming Fear of Failure
description: Learn how to embrace mistakes as part of your learning journey.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama sat under the neem tree, her laptop battery dead, the power out in the neighborhood. Dumsor had struck again. She felt like the world was conspiring against her. Her code was half-finished and full of errors. She wanted to slam the lid and go to sleep. But she remembered a saying: 'The path to the market has thorns, but we still go to buy food.' She opened her notebook instead.[/STORY]

sections.heading: The Growth Mindset
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama used to think: 'I am either good at computers or I am not.' When she struggled, she thought she was failing. Akosua taught her about Carol Dweck's research. 'A growth mindset means you believe you can develop your brain through effort. If you fail, it just means you haven't succeeded YET.' Ama repeated that word: YET. It felt like a promise.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A growth mindset is the belief that abilities can be developed through dedication and hard work. Shifting from a fixed mindset to a growth mindset helps you embrace challenges. Replace 'I cannot code' with 'I cannot code YET.' Effort is the path to mastery.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Rewrite this fixed mindset thought into a growth mindset thought: 'I am terrible at JavaScript.'[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Bugs are Normal
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama learned that professional developers spend most of their time fixing errors, not writing new code. 'So I'm not slow?' she asked Akosua. 'No,' Akosua said, 'debugging is the job. Thomas Edison found 10,000 ways that didn't work before he made the bulb.' Ama felt a weight lift from her shoulders. If errors were normal, she didn't need to fear them.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Bugs and errors are normal parts of the programming cycle. Professional developers spend up to 80% of their time debugging. Treat error messages as useful guides rather than signs of failure. The process is simple: read, analyze, search, fix, and repeat.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Create a 3-step checklist of what you will do when you get stuck on a coding bug.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Overcoming Shame of Mistakes
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama was afraid to ask questions in the community forum. She thought, 'If I ask, they will think I am stupid.' Akosua showed her that even senior engineers ask questions on StackOverflow. 'Mistakes are just data,' Akosua said. Ama finally posted her bug. Within ten minutes, another student replied with the solution. Ama felt a warm wave of relief.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Stereotype threat can make women feel extra pressure to be perfect to avoid confirming negative stereotypes. Let go of perfectionism. The fastest way to learn is to break things, make mistakes, and ask for help in your community. Vulnerability is a strength in engineering.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Post one question or share one error you solved in the community forum. How did it feel to share?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Ghanaian Tech Community Resilience
sections.body:
[STORY]When the power cut, Ama didn't stop. She saved her work, closed her laptop, and read her offline PDF study notes on her phone. When she had slow internet, she downloaded resources at a local cafe and practiced coding offline at home. She realized that these obstacles were actually making her a more creative and resilient developer.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Resilience is key in Ghana's tech environment. Work within constraints: save work frequently (Ctrl+S), download learning materials for offline use, code offline using text editors, and connect with peers to share devices or internet hotspots.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]List two practical ways you can continue learning during a power outage or when you run out of mobile data.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What is a growth mindset?
options: The belief that intelligence is completely fixed
The belief that basic abilities can be developed through dedication and hard work
Focusing only on getting perfect grades
quiz.answer: The belief that basic abilities can be developed through dedication and hard work

quiz.question: In computer programming, bugs are:
options: A normal, everyday part of software development
quiz.answer: A normal, everyday part of software development

quiz.question: What percentage of time do professional developers spend fixing bugs and debugging?
quiz.answer: Up to 80%

quiz.question: Why do women sometimes struggle more with tech mistakes?
options: They are naturally worse at logic
They face social pressure to be perfect and fear confirming negative stereotypes
They do not care about coding
quiz.answer: They face social pressure to be perfect and fear confirming negative stereotypes

quiz.question: How should you respond to a power outage or internet cut in Ghana?
options: Adapt, use offline capabilities, save code regularly, and stay calm
Complain to your mentor without taking action
quiz.answer: Adapt, use offline capabilities, save code regularly, and stay calm

title: Your Vision and Goals
description: Create a clear vision for your technology career and set achievable goals.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama stood on the hill overlooking Kumasi, looking at the city lights. She wanted a better life for her family and a career she was proud of. But the path felt foggy. Akosua told her, 'To get where you want to go, you need a map. A developer without a vision is like a driver without a destination.' Ama took out a clean sheet of paper to draw her map.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Defining Your Career Vision
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama asked herself: 'What do I want to build?' She thought of the local weavers in her village who made beautiful kente cloth but struggled to sell it. 'I want to build a website that lets anyone in the world buy kente directly from our weavers,' she wrote. Her vision was no longer just about 'learning computers' — it was about helping her community.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A clear vision connects your learning to a larger purpose, providing motivation during difficult lessons. Define what you want to achieve with technology — whether it is building a local business site, creating a mobile app, or supporting your family.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write down your tech vision. What is the ultimate project or career goal you want to achieve?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: SMART Goal Framework
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wrote: 'I want to learn HTML.' Akosua shook her head. 'Too broad. Make it SMART.' Ama rewrote it: 'I will write HTML code for 30 minutes every morning at 6 AM before heading to the market, and build one sample page by Friday.' This goal felt real. She knew exactly what she had to do and when.[/STORY]

[LESSON]SMART goals are Specific, Measurable, Actionable, Relevant, and Time-bound. Instead of vague desires, define precise actions. This keeps you focused and prevents overwhelm, allowing you to build technical skills systematically.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write one SMART goal for your learning this week. Make sure it has a specific time and a measurable outcome.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Action Plans and Habits
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama's house was noisy during the day with chores and visitors. She decided to wake up at 5:00 AM, when the air was cool and the compound was quiet. She sat at the table with her notebook and code editor. Those 45 minutes of quiet focus became her favorite part of the day. She was amazed at how much she learned when there were no distractions.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Create a dedicated study routine that fits your lifestyle. Protect this time by setting expectations with your family. Even 30-45 minutes of daily, uninterrupted focus is more effective than hours of distracted studying once a week.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]What is the best time and place for you to study without distractions? How will you protect this time?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Sharing Your Goals
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama made a simple chart on her wall with 20 boxes. Every time she completed a module, she colored in a box with a red pen. Ticking off the boxes gave her a sense of progress. When she looked at the chart and saw 5 colored boxes, she realized she was no longer a complete beginner. She was moving forward.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Visualizing your progress builds momentum and fights imposter syndrome. Keep a physical or digital milestone tracker. Ticking off completed tasks reinforces your mastery and reminds you how far you have come.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Create a physical tracker in your notebook or on your wall to mark your progress through these 20 modules.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What does the S in SMART stand for?
quiz.answer: Specific

quiz.question: Which of the following is a SMART goal?
options: I want to get rich in technology
I want to study web development some day
I will code for 45 minutes every Monday and Wednesday at 8 AM for the next 6 weeks
I will try my best to learn Python
quiz.answer: I will code for 45 minutes every Monday and Wednesday at 8 AM for the next 6 weeks

quiz.question: Daily micro-habits should be:
options: Done only when you feel inspired
quiz.answer: Small, realistic, and consistent actions

title: Community and Support Systems
description: Build your support network and learn how community accelerates growth.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama was stuck on a CSS layout. No matter what she did, the image would not center. She felt isolated and frustrated, ready to throw her notes away. Akosua saw her. 'Ama, why are you carrying this weight alone? The best developers in the world code in teams. You need a support network.' Ama realized she didn't have to walk this road by herself.[/STORY]

sections.heading: The Power of Peer Networks
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama used to think programmers were lone geniuses working in dark rooms. Akosua explained: 'Modern software is too large for one person. Teams at MTN or Hubtel use collaboration tools to write code together. Communication and helping others is just as important as writing logic.' Ama understood that learning to collaborate was a key skill.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Software engineering is a collaborative team sport, not a solo activity. Modern platforms are built by teams who share code, review designs, and solve bugs together. Building strong communication and teamwork skills is essential for a professional tech career.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]What is one way you can practice teamwork in your daily life that can translate to a tech career?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Mentorship is a Catalyst
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama reached out to Esi and Beatrice, two other girls in the Pool of Grace program. They created a WhatsApp group called 'Kumasi Dev Girls.' They promised to share their bugs and study together. When Beatrice got stuck, Ama helped her. Helping Beatrice made Ama feel smart and helped her understand the lesson even better.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Peer learning is one of the fastest ways to learn. Set up small study circles with classmates. Explaining code to others reinforces your own understanding and builds a supportive community that keeps everyone accountable.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Reach out to at least one peer in this program and agree to check in on each other's progress weekly. Write down their name.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Constructive Online Forums
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama shared her website link in the group. Esi wrote back: 'I love the headers, but the button text is hard to read because the background is too dark.' Ama felt a brief sting of defensive pride, but she realized Esi was right. She changed the button color, and it looked much better. 'Thank you, Esi!' she wrote.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Constructive feedback is the mechanism of improvement in tech. Frame feedback not as criticism of your worth, but as support for your work. Welcome suggestions with an open mind, and give peer reviews that are specific and encouraging.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Review a peer's project in the forum. Write down one specific thing they did well and one constructive suggestion.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Ghana Tech Hubs
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama had a bug she couldn't solve for two hours. She wanted to message Akosua: 'My code is broken, help.' But she remembered Akosua's rule. She wrote instead: 'Hi Akosua, I am trying to center an image. I tried setting the margin to auto and changing display to block, but it is still aligned left. Here is my code.' Akosua replied in minutes, guiding her to the solution.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Mentors are here to guide your thinking, not write code for you. When asking for help, describe the problem, show the error, explain what you expected, and list what you have already tried. This shows initiative and helps the mentor help you.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Draft a help request for a current bug you are facing, following the structured format: problem, expectation, and attempts made.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: Why is a peer network important in tech?
options: They provide support, share learning resources, and decrease dropouts
quiz.answer: They provide support, share learning resources, and decrease dropouts

quiz.question: What is Kumasi Hive?
options: A technology hub providing workspace, community, and training
quiz.answer: A technology hub providing workspace, community, and training

options: State what you are trying to accomplish
Ask someone to do the whole task for you

quiz.answer: Ask someone to do the whole task for you

quiz.question: How do mentors help accelerate your career?
options: They navigate career choices, review work, and connect you to opportunities
They attend classes in your place
quiz.answer: They navigate career choices, review work, and connect you to opportunities

quiz.question: Which of the following is a tech hub based in Accra?
quiz.answer: iSpace / Mobile Web Ghana

title: Celebrating Your Progress
description: Recognize and celebrate your achievements on your technology journey.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama sat under the mango tree, her notebook open. She had completed all seven self-worth modules. She looked back at the first day when she sat on the bench, afraid of 'computer things.' Now, she felt a strong, quiet confidence. She had a growth mindset, a study routine, and a vision. She decided to celebrate this first victory with a cool bottle of Sobolo.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Celebrating the Process, Not Just Outcomes
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama felt guilty about resting. 'I should start the HTML module immediately,' she thought. But Akosua stopped her. 'No, Ama. Your brain needs to know when it has won. If you just run to the next task, you will burn out. Celebrate your hard work.' Ama closed her laptop and enjoyed the sweet, spicy taste of her Sobolo, feeling proud.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Celebrating milestones releases dopamine, reinforcing the learning behavior and building sustained motivation. Never skip celebrating your progress. Recognizing your successes builds resilience for the challenges ahead.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]How will you celebrate completing this first phase of the program? Choose a simple, meaningful reward.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Reinforcing Self-Efficacy
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama started a page in her notebook called 'Ama's Win Log.' She wrote: 'Module 1: I declared my worth. Module 2: I negotiated study time. Module 3: I rendered my name in HTML. Module 4: I solved a syntax error.' Looking at the list made her feel like a champion. Whenever she doubted herself, she would read the log.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A Win Log is a written record of your achievements, breakthroughs, and problems solved. Keeping this log helps you fight imposter syndrome and provides visual evidence of your growth when you encounter difficult technical challenges.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Create a Win Log page in your notebook and write down three wins you have achieved since starting this program.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Preparing for the Tech Transition
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama established a rule: after every 45-minute focus session, she would walk outside for 5 minutes and chat with her neighbor, or listen to her favorite song. After completing a full module, she would cook a special meal or rest. These small rewards kept her excited to sit down and study every day.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Tie healthy, non-destructive rewards directly to completing your milestones. This builds a positive feedback loop in your brain, linking the effort of coding with positive experiences, making learning sustainable.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]List three healthy, simple rewards you can use to treat yourself after completing coding sessions or modules.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: A Letter to Yourself
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama looked at her old charcoal declaration: 'I, Ama, can learn to code.' She smiled. She was no longer afraid of the red errors or the computer screens. She had built a solid mental house. She was ready to lay the technical bricks. She opened the HTML notes with excitement, ready for the next stage of her journey.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A solid psychological foundation is required before learning technical skills. By building self-worth, setting goals, establishing community, and reframing failure, you have prepared your mind for the technical challenges ahead. You are ready to write code.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Reflect on how your mindset has changed since Module 1. Write down one major shift in your thinking.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: Why should you celebrate small steps in learning?
options: To reinforce self-efficacy and build positive learning loops
So you can take a long vacation
It is a waste of time
quiz.answer: To reinforce self-efficacy and build positive learning loops

quiz.question: Completing the 7 self-worth modules means you are:
options: Psychologically prepared and ready for technical coding modules
Finished with the Pool of Grace course
quiz.answer: Psychologically prepared and ready for technical coding modules

quiz.question: When you feel discouraged during technical modules, you should:
options: Assume you were wrong about your capacity
Reflect on your progress and remember your original motivation
quiz.answer: Reflect on your progress and remember your original motivation

quiz.question: What is the focus of self-worth celebration?
quiz.answer: Acknowledge effort, progress, and resilience

quiz.question: Your self-worth journey:
options: Continues as a foundation as you build technical and career resources
quiz.answer: Continues as a foundation as you build technical and career resources

title: Introduction to HTML and CSS
description: Learn the building blocks of web development from absolute scratch.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama sat with her laptop open, ready to write her first web page. She wanted to build a page to showcase the beautiful kente cloth woven in her village. 'How do I start?' she asked. Akosua took a bowl of waakye. 'Think of HTML as the food dividers in this bowl, and CSS as the decorations on the table. Let's build the structure first.'[/STORY]

sections.heading: What is HTML?
sections.body:
[STORY]Akosye pointed to the waakye bowl. 'Look at how the leaf divides the rice from the beans, and the egg sits in its own corner. In HTML, we use tags to create dividers. `

` is your big stall banner, `

` is the description of your goods, and `` is the photo.' Ama typed `

Kente from Kumasi

` and watched it appear on the screen. She was building her first digital stall.[/STORY] [LESSON]HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the structure of a webpage. Tags tell the browser what type of content each element is. `

` is for main headings, `

` for paragraphs, and `` for links. Every website begins as an HTML skeleton.[/LESSON] [YOUR_TURN]Write a basic HTML line containing a paragraph tag with the sentence: 'I am learning HTML.'[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: What is CSS?
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama's page was plain black text on a white background. 'It looks dull,' she said. Akosua smiled. 'Time for CSS. Think of kente weaving — you choose yellow for royalty, blue for peace, and red for love. In CSS, we choose colors, fonts, and layouts to make the HTML look beautiful.' Ama added a CSS style to make her background a soft warm orange and her headers a deep purple. Her page instantly felt alive.[/STORY]

[LESSON]CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) controls the visual presentation of a webpage. You use CSS to apply colors, define fonts, add spacing, and set layouts. CSS separates the design layer from the HTML structure, giving you creative control.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a CSS rule that changes the color of all `<h1_>` tags to purple and sets their text alignment to center.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: HTML Document Structure
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wanted to add space around her text so it didn't touch the borders. Akosua explained the Box Model. 'Every element is a cardboard box. The text is the kente cloth inside. Padding is the bubble wrap inside the box. Border is the cardboard itself. Margin is the empty space between different boxes on the shelf.' Ama adjusted the padding and margin, and her layout looked neat and professional.[/STORY]

[LESSON]In CSS, every element is treated as a rectangular box. The CSS Box Model consists of: 1. Content (text/image), 2. Padding (space inside the border), 3. Border (the edge), and 4. Margin (space outside the border). Mastering this is key to layout design.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Draw a diagram of the CSS Box Model in your notebook, labeling Content, Padding, Border, and Margin.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Ghanaian CSS Styling Challenge
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama opened her text editor, saved a new file as `index.html`, and typed out the full structure: `<html>`, `<head>`, `<body>`. She linked a `style.css` file and added her kente details. She opened the file in her browser without any internet connection. It loaded perfectly! 'I built a webpage offline!' she cheered. Akosua nodded. 'You are now a creator.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]To build a webpage, create a folder with `index.html` and `style.css`. Link them together using the `<link>` tag in the head. You can test and view your webpage locally in any web browser without needing an active internet connection.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Create a simple HTML page on your computer with a heading, paragraph, and styled background. View it in your browser.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What does HTML stand for?
quiz.answer: Hyper Text Markup Language

quiz.question: Which HTML tag is used for the largest heading?
What does CSS stand for?
quiz.answer: Cascading Style Sheets

quiz.question: Which HTML element contains the visible content of a webpage?
quiz.answer: <body>

quiz.question: How do you apply a color to a text in CSS?
quiz.answer: color: blue;

title: JavaScript Fundamentals
description: Learn programming logic and JavaScript basics with hands-on exercises.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama had her beautiful kente page, but it was static. 'I want buyers to select a cloth size and see the price change instantly,' she told Akosua. Akosua tapped the laptop. 'For that, we need JavaScript. If HTML is the stall and CSS is the paint, JavaScript is the calculator and the helper who talks to customers.' Ama opened her script file, excited to add action.[/STORY]

sections.heading: What is JavaScript?
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama learned that JavaScript runs behind the scenes, reacting to what the user does. When a customer clicks a button, JavaScript catches the click, processes the logic, and updates the screen. Ama wrote a simple script that popped up a message: 'Welcome to our Kente Stall!' when the page loaded. She felt like she had given her page a voice.[/STORY]

[LESSON]JavaScript is the programming language that makes webpages interactive. While HTML handles structure and CSS handles style, JavaScript handles dynamic behavior — responding to user clicks, calculating numbers, updating content, and validating forms.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]What is the role of JavaScript compared to HTML and CSS? Write a one-sentence summary.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Variables and Data Types
sections.body:
[STORY]Akosua showed Ama how to store data. 'Think of a market woman's record book. She writes down: customerName = Ama, kentePrice = 350. In JavaScript, we do the same using variables.' Ama declared her first variables: `let customer = 'Ama';` and `let price = 350;`. She understood that variables were just labeled baskets holding information.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Variables are containers for storing data values. In modern JavaScript, we declare them using `let` (for values that can change) and `const` (for values that stay the same). Data types include strings (text), numbers, booleans (true/false), and arrays (lists).[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write two lines of JavaScript: one declaring a variable for your age, and another for your name.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Conditional Logic
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wanted to offer a discount. 'If the customer buys more than two cloths, give a 10% discount,' she planned. Akosua showed her the `if/else` statement. Ama coded: `if (quantity > 2) { price = price * 0.9; } else { price = price; }`. She ran the script with different quantities and watched the prices adjust. It was like magic, but it was pure logic.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Control flow allows your program to make decisions based on conditions. The `if/else` statement executes one block of code if a condition is true, and a different block if it is false. This logic makes your application interactive and smart.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a JavaScript condition that checks if a user's age is 18 or older, and logs 'Adult' if true, or 'Minor' if false.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Loops and Functions
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama had to calculate the total price for different customers. Instead of writing the discount logic over and over, Akosua showed her how to write a function. 'Think of Jollof rice,' Akosua said. 'You write the recipe once as a function, then you just call it with different ingredients.' Ama wrote a function `calculateTotal(price, qty)` and called it three times. It saved time and kept her code clean.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A function is a reusable block of code designed to perform a specific task. You define the function once (with inputs called parameters), and then you can execute ('call') it multiple times with different values, returning a result.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a JavaScript function named `add` that takes two numbers as parameters and returns their sum.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: How do you declare a variable that cannot be reassigned?
quiz.answer: const myVar = 5;

quiz.question: Which keyword is used to create conditional logic in JavaScript?
quiz.answer: if

quiz.question: What data type represents true or false values?
quiz.answer: Boolean

quiz.question: How do you write a comment in JavaScript?
quiz.answer: // Comment

quiz.question: What is the purpose of a function?
options: To group reusable code together to perform a task
quiz.answer: To group reusable code together to perform a task

title: Building Your First Website
description: Put your HTML CSS and JavaScript skills together to build a real website.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama stood back and looked at her project folder. She had `index.html`, `style.css`, and `script.js` ready. 'Now we assemble them,' Akosua said. 'We will build a fully responsive, interactive kente calculator that fits on any phone in Ghana, from Accra to Bolgatanga.' Ama felt a thrill of excitement. This was her first complete web product.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Project Setup and Structure
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama organized her files. She placed her HTML file in the root directory, her styles in a `css` folder, and her script in a `js` folder. She linked the stylesheet in the `<head>` and the script at the bottom of the `<body>`. Linking them correctly meant the browser loaded the structure, design, and logic together as one functional app.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A standard web project is organized with `index.html` at the root, linking external files: `<link rel='stylesheet' href='css/style.css'>` and `<script src='js/script.js'></script>`. Keeping files organized makes your codebase clean and maintainable.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Draw a folder structure showing where to place HTML, CSS, and JS files for a clean web project.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Responsive Design Principles
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama loaded her site on her brother's cheap phone. The text was tiny and the image was cut off. 'Oh no,' she said. Akosua showed her CSS media queries. 'We must design for all screens. Mobile first.' Ama added `@media (max-width: 600px)` rules, making the font larger and the kente image stack vertically. On the phone, the site now looked perfect and clean.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Responsive Web Design ensures webpages look good on all devices (phones, tablets, laptops). Use CSS media queries to apply styles based on the device width, and use relative units like percentages, `rem`, or flexbox layouts instead of fixed pixels.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a basic media query in CSS that changes the background color to light gray when the screen width is 600px or less.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: DOM Manipulation
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wanted her JavaScript to read the quantity input from the screen and update the price text. She learned to select elements using `document.getElementById()`. She wrote a click listener: `button.addEventListener('click', () => { ... })`. When she entered '3' and clicked 'Calculate', the total changed from '0 GHS' to '945 GHS' right before her eyes.[/STORY]

[LESSON]The DOM (Document Object Model) is the browser's representation of your HTML page. JavaScript can manipulate the DOM by selecting elements, listening for user events (like clicks or keystrokes), changing text content, or modifying CSS styles in real time.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a JavaScript code snippet that selects an HTML element with the ID 'title' and changes its text to 'Welcome!'[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Ghanaian Small Business Showcase
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama opened her browser's developer console (Inspect element). She saw a red error message. Instead of panicking, she read it carefully: 'Cannot read property of null on line 15.' She realized she had misspelled the button ID. She fixed it, refreshed, and the console was clean. She tested the site offline multiple times. It worked flawlessly.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Use browser developer tools (Right-click -> Inspect -> Console) to test and debug your code. The console displays JavaScript outputs and syntax errors, helping you pinpoint and resolve issues quickly. Testing locally requires no internet connection.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Open your browser's Developer Tools on any page. Locate the console tab and run a simple `console.log('Test')` command. Write down what happens.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What does DOM stand for?
quiz.answer: Document Object Model

quiz.question: How do you link an external CSS file in HTML?
quiz.answer: <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">

quiz.question: Which CSS rule makes layouts adapt to mobile screens?
quiz.answer: @media query

quiz.question: How do you select an element by ID in JavaScript?
quiz.answer: document.getElementById("myId")

quiz.question: Where is the correct place to write metadata and stylesheet links?
quiz.answer: Inside <head>

title: Introduction to Python
description: Learn Python programming from scratch with practical Ghanaian examples.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama had built a frontend web page, but Akosua had a new challenge. 'Websites are great for users, but what if you need to process thousands of transactions or write server logic? For that, we need Python. It is a language of logic, data, and power. It is clean and readable, like writing in a clear voice.' Ama opened a new terminal, ready to learn.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Why Learn Python?
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama looked at a Python script. There were no brackets or semicolons like in JavaScript. Instead, it used simple indentations (spaces). Code looked like: `if score > 80:
    print('Pass')`. Ama loved it. It was clean and readable. Akosua explained: 'Python is used by companies like MTN for data analysis, and by Google for web servers. It is the language of problem solvers.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language known for its clean readability and simple syntax. Instead of brackets, Python uses indentation to define code blocks. It is widely used in web backend development, data science, automation, and AI.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a single line of Python code that prints: 'Hello, Ghana!'[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Variables and Simple Calculations
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wanted to list the colors of kente cloth. In Python, she created a list: `colors = ['yellow', 'red', 'green']`. Then she wanted to store the price of each style. She created a dictionary: `prices = {'Adweneasa': 500, 'Obiako': 450}`. She saw that Python made it simple to stack and categorize data, like stocking shelves at a store.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Python data structures include Lists (ordered, modifiable sequences), Dictionaries (unordered key-value mappings for lookups), and Tuples (ordered, unchangeable sequences). They allow developers to store and organize complex information efficiently.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write Python code declaring a dictionary named `student` with keys 'name' and 'score', and assign appropriate values.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Control Flow (If, For, While)
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama had a list of 5 sales: `sales = [350, 400, 500, 300, 450]`. She wanted to calculate the total revenue. She wrote a `for` loop: `total = 0
for sale in sales:
    total += sale`. She ran the script and it printed the sum instantly. She realized loops were like a persistent market assistant, counting every basket one by one without getting tired.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Loops allow you to repeat a block of code multiple times. A `for` loop iterates over a sequence (like a list or dictionary) item by item. A `while` loop continues running as long as a specified condition is true. Loops are essential for processing collections of data.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a Python `for` loop that iterates through a list of numbers `[1, 2, 3]` and prints each number multiplied by 2.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: List Operations and Dictionaries
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wrote a function to calculate tax on her sales. She typed: `def calculate_tax(amount):
    return amount * 0.05`. She passed 500 GHS into it, and it returned 25 GHS. She liked how she could wrap logic inside a clear name using the `def` keyword. Her code felt organized and reusable.[/STORY]

[LESSON]In Python, you define functions using the `def` keyword, followed by the function name, parameters, and a colon. The function body must be indented, and values are returned using `return`. Python functions promote code reusability and clarity.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a Python function named `greet` that takes a name as a parameter and returns: 'Hello, ' + name.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: How does Python define blocks of code?
quiz.answer: Using indentation (whitespace)

quiz.question: Which keyword defines a function in Python?
quiz.answer: def

quiz.question: What data structure stores key-value pairs in Python?
quiz.answer: Dictionary

quiz.question: How do you print text in Python?
quiz.answer: print("text")

quiz.question: Which of the following is a valid Python list?
quiz.answer: my_list = [1, 2, 3]

title: Databases and SQL
description: Understand how data is stored and learn to query databases using SQL.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama realized that when she refreshed her web page or closed her Python terminal, all her data disappeared. 'How do we make our information stay forever?' she asked. Akosua took a thick leather ledger book. 'This is how market women record credits. In tech, we use databases. They are digital ledger books that remember everything, even when the power goes off.'[/STORY]

sections.heading: Relational Database Concepts
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama opened her notebook and drew columns for Customer ID, Name, Phone, and Order. Akosua showed her that this was a relational database table. 'We link tables together,' Akosua said. 'We have a Customers table and an Orders table, connected by a Customer ID. This prevents us from writing the same name over and over, keeping our ledger clean.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]A relational database stores data in structured tables consisting of rows (records) and columns (attributes). Tables are connected to each other using relationships established by matching identifiers called Primary Keys (unique table IDs) and Foreign Keys (links).[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Draw two related tables on paper: 'Students' and 'Grades', showing how they connect using a student ID. Write down the matching rows.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Writing SELECT Queries
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wanted to talk to the database. She learned SQL (Structured Query Language). She wrote her first command: `SELECT name FROM customers;`. It felt like writing a request to her market clerk: 'Give me the names from the customers book.' The database returned the list instantly. She felt a new sense of command over information.[/STORY]

[LESSON]SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language used to interact with relational databases. Core SQL commands include `SELECT` (retrieve data), `INSERT` (add records), `UPDATE` (modify data), and `DELETE` (remove records).[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write an SQL query that retrieves all columns from a table named `products`.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: CRUD Operations in SQL
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama only wanted to see orders from customers in Kumasi. She learned to add filter words. She wrote: `SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Kumasi' ORDER BY name ASC;`. The query filtered the list to only Kumasi users and sorted them alphabetically. It was like magic, filtering a mountain of records in a fraction of a second.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Refine SQL queries using the `WHERE` clause to filter rows based on specific conditions (operators like `=`, `>`, `LIKE`). Use `ORDER BY` followed by `ASC` (ascending) or `DESC` (descending) to sort the output, making data easy to analyze.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write an SQL query that selects names from the `users` table where the score is greater than 80, sorted by score in descending order.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Joining Tables
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wanted to see the names of customers alongside the items they ordered. Since names were in one table and orders in another, she used a `JOIN`. She wrote: `SELECT customers.name, orders.item FROM customers JOIN orders ON customers.id = orders.customer_id;`. She saw the customer name and order item side by side. The database had bridged the tables perfectly.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A `JOIN` clause combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them (typically foreign keys). Joining tables allows you to query normalized database structures to produce comprehensive, readable reports.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a basic query outline joining a `students` table and a `courses` table based on a shared `student_id` column.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What does SQL stand for?
quiz.answer: Structured Query Language

quiz.question: Which SQL statement is used to retrieve data from a database?
quiz.answer: SELECT

quiz.question: What is a Primary Key?
options: A password used to access a database
A unique identifier for each record in a table
The first column in any database table
quiz.answer: A unique identifier for each record in a table

quiz.question: Which SQL keyword is used to filter records?
quiz.answer: WHERE

quiz.question: What SQL command is used to add new rows to a table?
quiz.answer: INSERT INTO

title: Web Development Project
description: Build a complete web application project for your portfolio.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama looked at her skills: HTML/CSS for visual layout, JavaScript for client interaction, Python for server operations, and SQL for databases. 'Now,' Akosua said, 'we connect them all. We will build a Full Stack application. It is the ultimate system where the front counter talks to the kitchen, and the kitchen gets food from the pantry.' Ama sat straight, ready for the challenge.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Full-Stack Architecture
sections.body:
[STORY]Akosua explained the three layers. 'Frontend is the front counter where the customer stands. Backend is the kitchen where the chef cooks. Database is the pantry where ingredients are stored. When a customer orders kente, the frontend sends the order to the backend, the backend updates the database pantry, and confirms the sale.' Ama realized how the pieces fit together.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Full Stack development refers to working on both the client-side (Frontend: user interface) and server-side (Backend: server logic, APIs, and Database) of an application. Understanding how these layers communicate is essential for building modern web applications.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Sketch a diagram showing the flow of a user request from the Frontend to the Backend, then to the Database, and back.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Form Handlers and State
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wondered how the frontend sent requests to the backend. 'We use an API,' Akosua said. 'Think of a waiter. The waiter takes your order from the table (frontend), carries it to the kitchen (backend), and brings the food back. The API is the digital waiter that carries JSON data back and forth.' Ama understood: APIs were the bridges of the internet.[/STORY]

[LESSON]An API (Application Programming Interface) is a set of definitions and protocols that allows different software applications to communicate. Web APIs typically use HTTP requests to exchange structured data, usually in JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) format, between frontend and backend.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]In your own words, describe what a Web API does in a full-stack application.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Ghanaian Community Resource Directory
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama used Node.js and Express to write backend server code. She wrote a route: `app.get('/api/kente', (req, res) => { res.json(kenteData); });`. She started the server, opened her browser to localhost:5000/api/kente, and saw her data text load. She had built her first server! It was running locally on her computer, listening and waiting to serve requests.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Express is a minimal web framework for Node.js. It allows developers to set up servers that listen on specific network ports, define API routes (endpoints like `/api/data`), handle requests, process logic, and return responses to client applications.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a basic code block setting up an Express server route for `/api/hello` that returns a simple JSON message.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Deployment Planning
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama went to her frontend JavaScript file. She used the `fetch()` function to call her new backend endpoint: `fetch('/api/kente').then(res => res.json()).then(data => { display(data); });`. She refreshed her webpage, and the data from her Express server appeared on her HTML screen. She had completed the link. She was officially a Full Stack developer![/STORY]

[LESSON]To connect frontend and backend, use the browser's `fetch()` API or library calls to make asynchronous HTTP requests to backend routes. The frontend receives the JSON data, updates the React state or DOM elements, and displays the content to the user.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a simple JavaScript `fetch` call that requests data from `/api/status` and logs the result to the browser console.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What does a full-stack application include?
options: Both frontend and backend components including a database
quiz.answer: Both frontend and backend components including a database

quiz.question: Which library is commonly used in React to make API calls?
quiz.answer: axios

quiz.question: What is the purpose of REST APIs?
options: To allow communication between frontend client and backend server using HTTP methods
quiz.answer: To allow communication between frontend client and backend server using HTTP methods

quiz.question: What does the abbreviation URL mean?
quiz.answer: Uniform Resource Locator

quiz.question: In web development, state refers to:
options: The geographic region of the server
An object that holds component data that can change over time
quiz.answer: An object that holds component data that can change over time

title: Version Control with GitHub
description: Learn how to use GitHub to manage and share your code professionally.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama was proud of her full-stack weavers application. But she wanted to work with Esi on it. 'If Esi changes a style file and I change the logic, how do we combine our work without deleting each other's code?' she asked. Akosua opened a terminal. 'For that, we use Git and GitHub. It is a digital time machine and a global marketplace where developers share their creations.'[/STORY]

sections.heading: Introduction to Git
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama remembered when she had copy-pasted her files as 'index_final.html', 'index_final_v2.html', and ended up confused. Akosua showed her Git. 'Git takes clean snapshots of your project folder. If you make a mistake, you can travel back in time to yesterday's snapshot. No more copy-pasting, and no more lost code.' Ama felt a wave of relief. She had a safety net.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Version control is a system that records changes to a file or set of files over time so that you can recall specific versions later. Git is the most popular open-source distributed version control system, allowing developers to track modifications and revert code easily.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Why is version control better than manually duplicating files with names like 'code_version2'? Write down your thoughts.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Core Git Commands
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama initialized her project folder. She typed `git init`, then `git add .` to select her files, and `git commit -m 'Initial weavers project commit'` to save the snapshot. Finally, she typed `git push` to upload the snapshot to her online account on GitHub. She saw her project repository visible online. It was safe from any computer crash.[/STORY]

[LESSON]The core Git workflow has three stages: 1. Working Directory (making changes), 2. Staging Area (`git add` to select changes to save), and 3. Local Repository (`git commit` to save the snapshot). Use `git push` to upload local commits to remote hosts like GitHub.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write down the exact three Git command lines you use to save your local changes and upload them to GitHub.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: GitHub Collaboration
sections.body:
[STORY]Esi wanted to change the buttons to yellow. Ama created a new Git branch called 'esi-buttons' and gave Esi access. Esi wrote her CSS on that branch, while Ama continued coding database routes on the 'main' branch. When Esi finished, Ama merged Esi's branch into main. The buttons changed, the logic remained, and nothing broke. It was seamless teamwork.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Branches in Git allow developers to work on new features, bug fixes, or experiments in isolation from the main codebase. Once the work is tested and approved, the branch is merged back into the 'main' branch, enabling safe parallel collaboration.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Explain how branching helps two developers collaborate on the same file without overwriting each other's work.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Creating an Open Source Profile
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama looked at her GitHub dashboard. She saw the green boxes showing her daily coding commits. Akosua told her, 'Your GitHub profile is your modern CV. Companies in Accra like Hubtel and MTN look at your repositories to see if you can write clean, organized code.' Ama wrote a friendly readme page describing her weavers project, showing she was ready for professional work.[/STORY]

[LESSON]GitHub hosts code repositories and serves as a portfolio for developers. A clean profile with active commit history ('green squares'), well-documented README files, and clean repository folders is the strongest technical resume for landing a job.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Create your GitHub account, set up a profile picture, and write a short bio stating your interest in web development.[/YOUR_TURN]

title: Ghana Tech Job Market
description: Discover technology career opportunities and salary expectations in Ghana.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama had built a solid technical foundation. She knew how to build responsive interfaces, server API routes, and database tables. She felt a spark of ambition. 'I am ready to find a job,' she told Akosua. Akosua took a map of Ghana and pointed to Accra. 'Let's look at the tech ecosystem. There are opportunities waiting, but you must know how to navigate the market.'[/STORY]

sections.heading: Sector Overview
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama learned about the hubs and companies in Ghana. She researched companies like MTN, Vodafone, Hubtel, Zeepay, and mPharma. Akosua told her: 'Accra is the heart of tech, but Kumasi is rising. You don't have to build the next Facebook; you can start by supporting small businesses or working as an intern in a local startup.' Ama realized the market was real and within reach.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Ghana's tech ecosystem is growing, driven by mobile payment platforms, e-commerce startups, and multinational support services. Main hubs include Meltwater Entrepreneurial School of Technology (MEST), Soronko Academy, and DevCongress Ghana. Opportunities exist in junior development, IT support, and QA testing.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]List three active technology companies in Ghana and name one product or service they offer.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Common Job Profiles
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wondered what job titles she could apply for. Akosua explained: 'Frontend developers build visual designs. Backend developers write logic and database tables. Full stack developers do both. Quality Assurance (QA) engineers test software for errors. Data Analysts inspect sales numbers.' Ama saw that she could choose a role that fit her favorite skills.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Common roles in the tech market include: Frontend Developer, Backend Developer, QA Engineer, UX Designer, and Data Analyst. Entry-level candidates often start as interns, junior developers, or technical support specialists to gain practical experience.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Based on the modules you have completed, which role (frontend, backend, or QA) excites you most? Why?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Compensation Details
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama was curious about salaries. Akosua shared: 'A junior developer in a Ghanaian startup can expect between 2,500 GHS and 5,000 GHS per month. In larger tech firms, it can be higher. Remote international jobs pay even more, but you must build experience first.' Ama computed the numbers. It was enough to support her family and invest in her own growth. It felt like a dream she could achieve.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Junior developer salaries in Ghana typically range from 2,000 GHS to 5,000 GHS per month for local companies, depending on size, location, and role. Building specialized skills (like React or Django) and gaining experience increases earning potential rapidly.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Calculate a basic monthly budget showing how an entry-level tech salary of 3,000 GHS could help support your studies and family. Write it down.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Empirical Context
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama thought that completing her coding course meant she was done learning. Akosua corrected her: 'Technology never stops. Languages change, frameworks update. The best developers are lifelong learners. You should dedicate at least 3-5 hours a week to reading articles, coding projects, and learning new libraries.' Ama nodded, accepting the marathon of growth.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Continuous learning is mandatory in software engineering due to rapid technology cycles. Upskilling strategies include: allocating dedicated weekly hours for self-study, reading tech blogs, contributing to open source, and building personal side projects to stay relevant.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Design a simple weekly learning schedule showing when you will spend time upskilling after completing these modules.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What is the average growth rate of Ghana's tech sector?
quiz.answer: 18% annually

quiz.answer: Frontend Developer
