title: Building Your CV and Portfolio
description: Create a professional CV and portfolio that showcases your tech skills.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama had her resume ready, but it looked like a school report. It listed her classes, her grade, and her high school. Akosua took the document and shook her head. 'Ama, a tech resume is not about where you sat in school. It is about what you can build. Let's rebuild your CV to show off your kente shop application and your Git commits.' Ama sat at the screen, ready to transform her profile.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Structure of a Developer CV
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama removed her high school grades and added a 'Projects' section at the top. She listed: 'Full Stack Kente Cloth Store: Built a responsive calculator in HTML/CSS/JS, connected to an Express API and PostgreSQL database, tracking transactions offline.' She read it back. It sounded like the description of a real engineer, not just a student. She felt proud of her achievements.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A tech CV should prioritize practical projects, developer skills (HTML, CSS, Git, SQL), and live links over general academic history. Describe projects using action verbs and clearly state the technologies used, focusing on the problems you solved.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Draft the 'Projects' section of your CV, describing the weavers' project or another project you want to build.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Building an Online Portfolio
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama bought a cheap local domain name and hosted her portfolio website online. The page had a clean design: 'Hello, I'm Ama, a full-stack developer in Kumasi.' It featured screenshots of her kente store, links to her GitHub profile, and a button to download her CV. She shared the link with Akosua. 'This is your storefront,' Akosua said. 'It shows you are professional.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]A developer portfolio is a personal website showcasing your skills, projects, and contact info. It serves as your primary digital presence, proving you can design and deploy clean, functional web projects to the public internet.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Create a basic design mock-up of your portfolio site in your notebook, labeling the main sections: About, Projects, and Contact.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Writing Project Summaries
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama had built 5 small sandbox pages during her training. Akosua advised her: 'Only showcase your best 2 projects. Employers have very little time. Make sure those 2 are completely polished, responsive, and document their features clearly on GitHub.' Ama selected her kente shop and her python inventory calculator, making them as perfect as possible.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Quality is better than quantity in portfolios. Select 2-3 of your best, fully completed projects to showcase. Ensure they are clean, mobile-responsive, and have functional, bug-free interactive features that demonstrate your capabilities.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Identify two projects you have built or want to build that you will display on your portfolio website. Write down their core features.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Proofreading and Polish
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama opened the README file for her weavers project on GitHub. She wrote a clear description: 'This application helps local Ghanaian weavers display their kente styles and calculate prices offline.' She added installation instructions, screenshots, and listed the tech stack: React, Express, Node.js, PostgreSQL. She realized it looked professional and easy to understand.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Every repository on GitHub needs a README.md file. It acts as the project manual, explaining: 1. Project Purpose, 2. Technologies Used, 3. How to install and run the code, and 4. Project features. A good readme demonstrates communication and organization skills.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a draft README for one of your GitHub repositories, detailing the project overview and installation steps.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What is the most critical link to include on a developer CV?
quiz.answer: Your GitHub profile link

quiz.question: An online portfolio should demonstrate:
options: Interactive links to projects you have built with their source code
All the books you have read
quiz.answer: Interactive links to projects you have built with their source code

quiz.question: How should you describe projects?
options: Detail the problem solved, tech used, and your implementation steps
State that the code is private
Let employers guess what it does
quiz.answer: Detail the problem solved, tech used, and your implementation steps

title: Interview Preparation
description: Prepare for technology job interviews with practice questions and techniques.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama's phone rang. It was the hiring manager from Hubtel in Accra. 'Ama, we saw your portfolio and your kente calculator on GitHub. We would like to invite you for a junior developer interview next Tuesday.' Ama gasped, her heart pounding with a mixture of joy and panic. She hung up and looked at Akosua. 'I am terrified,' she said. Akosua smiled. 'Time to prepare.'[/STORY]

sections.heading: The Tech Interview Process
sections.body:
[STORY]Akosua mocked an interview. 'Tell me about a time you faced a difficult coding error.' Ama hesitated, then used the STAR method. 'Situation: I was building my kente calculator when the DOM script broke. Task: I had to get the total price to update. Action: I used browser inspect tools, found the ID misspelling, and fixed it. Result: The page loaded correctly and user score rose.' Akosua nodded. 'Perfect.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]Use the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result) to answer behavioral interview questions. Describe a specific challenge you faced, what you had to do, the actions you took to resolve it, and the positive outcomes achieved.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Draft an answer to the question: 'Tell me about a time you struggled with a project and how you handled it', following the STAR structure.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Handling Coding Exercises
sections.body:
[STORY]Akosua gave Ama a whiteboard challenge: write a function that reverses a string. Ama felt her mind go blank under the pressure. 'Take a deep breath,' Akosua whispered. 'Explain your thinking out loud.' Ama spoke: 'I will start by splitting the string into an array, reverse the array, and join it back.' She wrote the code, explaining each step. Even when she made a minor typo, the manager guided her because they saw her logical process.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Technical interviews test your coding logic. Practice explaining your thought process out loud as you write code. Interviewers want to see how you dissect problems, structured thinking, and how you receive feedback, not just perfect memory of syntax.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Practice solving a simple programming task (like finding the largest number in a list) out loud, explaining each step to an imaginary interviewer.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: The STAR Method for Behavioral Questions
sections.body:
[STORY]The interviewer asked Ama: 'How would you design a database to track sales for a market?' Ama closed her eyes, visualizing the relational database tables. 'I would create a Customers table, a Sales table, and link them using a customer_id key,' she explained, drawing the schemas. The interviewer smiled. Ama was thinking like a system architect, not just a coder.[/STORY]

[LESSON]For junior roles, understand system design basics: how database tables link (one-to-many, many-to-many relationships), how clients fetch data from backend APIs, how servers manage data states, and how security or caching is structured at a high level.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Sketch a database design for a library management system: show tables for Books, Authors, and Borrowers, and their relationships.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Asking Questions at the End
sections.body:
[STORY]At the end of the interview, the manager asked Ama: 'Do you have any questions for us?' Ama asked: 'What technologies is the team planning to adopt this year, and how do you support learning for junior developers?' The manager's eyes lit up. Ama's questions showed she was ambitious, eager to learn, and interested in their team growth.[/STORY]

[LESSON]At the end of an interview, always ask 2-3 questions that show initiative and interest. Ask about the team's tech stack, their current challenges, the onboarding process, or how they foster professional development within the company.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write down three questions you will ask an interviewer at a technology company to show your interest in their team.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What does STAR stand for in behavioral interviews?
quiz.answer: Situation, Task, Action, Result

quiz.question: If you get stuck during a live coding interview, you should:
options: Quietly stop typing and wait for it to end
Explain your thought process out loud and ask clarifying questions
quiz.answer: Explain your thought process out loud and ask clarifying questions

quiz.question: What is a good question to ask the interviewer?
options: When do I get a raise?
What technologies does the engineering team use and what are the main goals?
quiz.answer: What technologies does the engineering team use and what are the main goals?

title: Networking and LinkedIn
description: Build your professional network and create a strong LinkedIn presence.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama had completed her interviews and was waiting for the response. Akosua told her, 'While you wait, don't sit in the dark. Build your network. Share your journey in public. The tech world is built on connections, and the best way to be seen is to show your work.' Ama logged onto LinkedIn, ready to share her story with the Ghanaian tech scene.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Your LinkedIn Profile
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama changed her LinkedIn headline to: 'Junior Full Stack Developer | React, Node.js, SQL | Pool of Grace Graduate.' She uploaded a friendly profile photo, wrote a summary about her passion for helping local artisans digitize their stores, and linked her portfolio website. Within a day, she had connection requests from recruiters in Accra.[/STORY]

[LESSON]LinkedIn is a primary recruiting channel in tech. Optimize your profile: write a clear, keyword-rich headline (skills/roles), add a professional photo, write a story-based summary, list your projects with links, and ask for recommendations from mentors.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Update your LinkedIn profile with your current developer skills and write a paragraph summary about your coding journey.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Connecting with Intention
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama attended a meeting of DevCongress Ghana. She was nervous walking into the crowded room. But she met Esi there, and they approached other developers. She introduced herself: 'Hi, I'm Ama, I just built a full-stack website for weavers.' Developers welcomed her, shared tips, and added her to their local tech channels. She was no longer just an outsider — she was part of the community.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Join local tech groups (DevCongress, Women in Tech Ghana, GDG Accra/Kumasi) and attend meetups or hackathons. Networking helps you learn about unadvertised job openings, find study partners, and get advice from senior developers.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Find and list one upcoming technology meetup or developer group in Ghana that you can join online or in person.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Tech Communities in Ghana
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama started posting small updates on LinkedIn once a week. She wrote: 'Today I learned how to use SQL joins to connect customer names with order files.' She posted a screenshot of her code. A senior developer commented: 'Great work, Ama! Keep it up.' Ama was amazed that her posts were reaching professional engineers. She was building her brand, one commit at a time.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Sharing your learning path in public builds your professional brand and credibility. Post about features you build, bugs you solve, or courses completed. This shows consistency, communication skills, and an active growth mindset to prospective employers.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Draft a short LinkedIn post describing a recent coding challenge you solved or a module you completed. Add relevant hashtags (e.g. #WomenInTech, #LearnInPublic).[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Attending Meetups
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wanted advice from a senior engineer at mPharma. She wrote a short message: 'Hi Abena, I admire your work at mPharma. I am a junior developer in Kumasi specializing in full-stack JavaScript. Can I ask you one question about how you prepare database designs?' Abena replied warmly: 'Hi Ama, absolutely. Let's chat next Wednesday.' Ama smiled. Direct, polite messages worked.[/STORY]

[LESSON]When reaching out to tech professionals on LinkedIn, write short, respectful messages: state who you are, what you admire about their work, keep requests specific and low-commitment (e.g. a 15-minute virtual chat or one question).[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a draft message to a Ghanaian tech professional whose career inspires you, requesting a short chat or asking one career question.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: What percentage of tech jobs are filled through networking?
quiz.answer: Over 70%

quiz.question: What is a strong LinkedIn headline for a graduate of this platform?
options: Frontend Developer | React & Python | Pool of Grace Graduate
quiz.answer: Frontend Developer | React & Python | Pool of Grace Graduate

quiz.question: How should you approach local tech meetups?

Attend, listen to talks, and exchange GitHub links with peers and speakers
quiz.answer: Attend, listen to talks, and exchange GitHub links with peers and speakers

title: Freelancing and Entrepreneurship
description: Explore freelancing opportunities and how to start your own tech business in Ghana.

content.intro: [STORY]A local pharmacy owner in Kumasi saw Ama's portfolio website. He approached her. 'Ama, I need a website that displays our drugs and allows customers to contact us. Can you build this for me? I will pay you.' Ama felt a wave of adrenaline. Her first paid client! She was entering the world of freelancing and entrepreneurship.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Freelancing Platforms
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama weighed her options. Akosua explained: 'Freelancing means you are your own boss, setting your own rates, and working on different projects. Full-time means steady salary, benefits, and team support. Freelancing is great to build your portfolio and earn income while you look for full-time roles.' Ama decided to accept the pharmacy project as a freelancer.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Freelancing offers flexibility, diverse project experience, and self-employment opportunities. Full-time employment offers income stability, structured mentoring, and company benefits. Many junior developers freelance to build experience before landing full-time positions.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]List two advantages and two challenges of working as a freelance developer in Ghana.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Setting Your Prices
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama set up a profile on Upwork, listing her skills: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Node.js. She looked at projects posted by clients worldwide. She wrote a personalized proposal: 'I can build a responsive landing page for you. Here is a link to my kente calculator.' She realized that global freelancing required clear communication, competitive rates, and proving your value quickly.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Freelancing platforms (Upwork, Fiverr, Toptal) allow developers to work on global projects. To succeed: create a highly targeted profile, write customized project proposals that address client problems directly, and start with competitive pricing to build high ratings.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Create a draft description for your Upwork freelance profile, highlighting your web development skills and project portfolio.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Managing Client Projects
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama walked through her neighborhood, looking at local schools, shops, and clinics. None of them had websites. She walked into a private school. 'Hi, I can build a simple website where parents can see term schedules and school fees. Let me show you a mockup.' The headmaster was impressed. Ama realized that local businesses in Ghana were a goldmine of freelance clients.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Local businesses (clinics, private schools, retail shops, logistics services) represent significant freelance opportunities in Ghana. Pitch your services by showing how a website can solve their business challenges: saving time, attracting customers, or explaining services.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Identify one local business in your community that does not have a website. Write a short pitch explaining how a website could improve their business.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Starting a Tech Business in Ghana
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama and the pharmacy owner agreed on a price of 2,000 GHS. Akosua advised her: 'Get it in writing. 30% deposit upfront, 40% when the design is approved, 30% when it goes live. Never start coding without a deposit.' The owner agreed and sent 600 GHS via Mobile Money (MoMo). Ama saw the money arrive. She felt proud and professional. She had her first contract.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Always use written contracts specifying project scope, deadline, and payment terms. Use milestone-based payments (e.g. upfront deposit, mid-project, final completion). Accept payments securely via local channels like Mobile Money (MoMo) or bank transfers.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a sample payment schedule for a project priced at 1,500 GHS, detailing the milestones and MoMo details for each payment.[/YOUR_TURN]

quiz.question: Which of the following is a popular freelancing platform?
quiz.answer: Upwork

quiz.question: To avoid scope creep, a freelancer should:
options: Do all extra work for free
Define project milestones and boundaries clearly in a written contract
quiz.answer: Define project milestones and boundaries clearly in a written contract

quiz.question: Where do you register a local business in Ghana?
quiz.answer: The Registrar General's Department

title: Continuing Your Tech Journey
description: Plan your next steps and discover advanced learning pathways in technology.

content.intro: [STORY]Ama sat at her laptop, the screen showing the message: 'Deploy complete.' Her pharmacy client website was live. She checked her bank account — the final MoMo payment had arrived. She had completed all 20 modules of Pool of Grace. She looked back to the first day when her aunt told her computers weren't for her. She had proven her aunt wrong. She had proven herself right. She was a software developer.[/STORY]

sections.heading: Lifelong Learning in Tech
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama asked Akosua: 'What do I learn next?' Akosua laughed. 'Your learning is just beginning. Use the 70-20-10 rule: 70% from building projects, 20% from code reviews and mentors, 10% from courses.' Ama made a schedule: 30 minutes of coding every weekday morning at 6:00 AM, and 1 hour of learning React on Saturdays. She was committed to the path.[/STORY]

[LESSON]A developer's learning never stops. Adopt the 70-20-10 learning rule: 70% of growth comes from doing/building, 20% from others/collaboration, and 10% from courses. Keep building projects, read documentation (MDN), and follow online tutorials regularly.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a personal coding and learning commitment: 'I will code for [number] minutes [number] days a week to continue my growth.'[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Choosing a Specialization
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama looked at different developer paths: React for frontend interfaces, Node.js/Express for backend logic, Python/pandas for data science, React Native for mobile apps. She decided to focus on Frontend development with React first. 'React is widely used in Accra,' Akosua agreed. 'Master it, and you will be highly competitive.'[/STORY]

[LESSON]After mastering foundational HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and database basics, choose a technical specialization: Frontend (React/TypeScript), Backend (Node.js/Django/SQL), Mobile development, or Data Science. Focusing on one track prevents distraction and accelerates career readiness.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Which technical track (frontend, backend, data science, mobile) do you choose to specialize in next? Why?[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Contributing to Open Source
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama wanted to contribute to the global tech ecosystem. She searched GitHub for 'good first issue' labels. She found a project translating basic web tutorials into local African languages. She contributed a translation in Twi. Her pull request was merged! Seeing her name in the list of contributors alongside developers from all over the world was a proud moment.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Contributing to open-source software on GitHub is a great way to learn professional workflows, collaborate globally, and gain visibility. Start small by fixing documentation typos, improving readmes, adding tests, or addressing beginner issues.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Find one open-source project on GitHub with a 'good first issue' label and write down its name and what the issue is asking for.[/YOUR_TURN]

sections.heading: Staying Connected to Pool of Grace
sections.body:
[STORY]Ama attended her final Saturday meeting. Agnes congratulated the graduating cohort. 'But don't disappear,' Agnes said. 'Become a peer mentor for the next cohort.' Ama raised her hand. She wanted to help other girls from Kumasi who were where she was 3 months ago. 'Obiara nnhyira ne ho' — Nobody blesses themselves alone. Ama was ready to give back.[/STORY]

[LESSON]Your connection to Pool of Grace continues as an alumna. Join the alumni directory, support the forum, become a peer mentor, and share your career successes. Giving back and teaching others is the ultimate way to reinforce your own skills and grow the tech community.[/LESSON]

[YOUR_TURN]Write a short message to the next cohort of girls starting Pool of Grace, sharing the most valuable lesson you learned about mindset and coding.[/YOUR_TURN]

options: Choose a technical specialization (like React or Django) and build complex projects
Wait for a company to train you from scratch
